_comment: Curated Q&A short-circuit for the Greater meta-bot. When a visitor's question semantically matches one of these `q` strings above the cosine threshold (0.78), the corresponding `a` is returned verbatim and no model inference happens. This is the demo-quality lever: it guarantees the suggested-prompt buttons and common follow-ups always get a hand-written answer, while the retrieval+model path still handles the long tail.

items.q: How does Greater work in plain English?
items.a: Greater is a free, open-source platform for support chatbots that run inside the visitor's browser. The first time you open a Greater-powered chat, your browser quietly downloads a small language model and an embedding model and caches them. After that, every question you type is answered locally on your device — there's no per-message API call to any vendor. The site you're talking to ships a curated knowledge bundle (a small JSON file of vetted text snippets); when you ask a question, the engine pulls the most relevant snippets and asks the in-browser model to write a grounded answer with inline citations. If the snippets don't cover your question, the bot says so plainly instead of guessing.

Greater is a sovereign, open-source support-bot platform. The shell is MIT-licensed and runs entirely in the visitor's browser via WebGPU. It ships with six industry persona templates (FinTech, Faith, Schools, Small Business, HealthTech, Startups) so you can see the same engine adapted to very different contexts. The site you're on is also the home page for hiring the author for a fractional engagement to build out the proprietary Pipes layer (curated knowledge bundles, persona-tuned weights, integrations) on top.

items.q: Is my chat data sent to the cloud?
items.a: No — by default, your chat content stays on your device. Greater runs the language model in WebGPU, in your browser. The text you type, the retrieved snippets, and the model's answer all live inside this tab — they are not sent to a server. The 'LOCAL · PRIVATE' badge in the chat header is a literal claim about that round trip: while it's green, your messages are not leaving the browser. Two things DO leave the browser, and the shell is honest about both: (1) the first time you open a Greater chat, your browser downloads the model files from a public CDN — that's a normal HTTPS file fetch, not message content, and the file is then cached; and (2) if you choose to escalate to a human via the contact form, your name, email, and message body are POSTed to the operator's contact endpoint. That escalation is opt-in and is the only path that ever sends your message to a server.

items.q: Where does my chat data go?
items.a: Your chat content stays on your device. The language model, the embedding model, and the retrieval index all run inside your browser tab. There is no per-message round trip to OpenAI, Anthropic, or any third-party LLM provider. The model files themselves are downloaded once from a public CDN and cached locally. The only outbound network call that ever carries your message text is the optional human-escalation contact form, which you trigger explicitly.

OpenClaw is the bring-your-own-LLM mode. By default, Greater downloads a small open-weights model into your browser via WebGPU. If you'd rather route the chat through a local model you're already running on your own machine — Ollama, LM Studio, llama.cpp's server, anything OpenAI-API-compatible — you can switch the chat into OpenClaw mode and point it at that endpoint. The shell stops downloading the in-browser model and instead sends each prompt to your local server. Your messages still don't leave your local network, because the endpoint you point at is yours. OpenClaw exists because the in-browser default has real tradeoffs: small models, slow first load, and limited reasoning. Operators or visitors who already have a 7B-or-bigger local model can get dramatically better answers without giving up the privacy story.

items.q: What bots ship out of the box?
items.a: Greater ships with six industry persona templates: (1) FinTech — the launch demo, answering real Bitcoin questions in your browser with no API key and no signup; (2) Faith — a confessional church support bot demonstrating explicit doctrinal posture; (3) Schools — an admissions and parent-questions bot for a small private school; (4) Small Business — a real-estate office front desk; (5) HealthTech — a member-services bot for a health-share community, including the explicit medical-advice refusal; and (6) Startups — a product-support bot for an early-stage SaaS. Each persona ships with its own knowledge bundle, its own tone prompt, and its own bias options. Every persona's knowledge is scoped to that persona — they don't bleed into each other.

items.q: Can I run this on my own domain?
items.a: Yes. The whole shell is MIT-licensed and lives at github.com/rorshockbtc/greater-than. Clone it, install dependencies with pnpm, replace the persona content under `artifacts/emerald/src/data/personas.ts` and the knowledge bundles under `artifacts/emerald/public/seeds/`, and deploy the static build to any host (Cloudflare Pages, Netlify, Vercel, your own NGINX). There is no proprietary backend you have to license to ship. The only optional server piece is the API server, which handles the human-escalation contact form and the admin feedback inbox; that's also in the repo. Forks get the FOSS shell plus the six persona scaffolds for free. What forks do NOT get is Pipes — the proprietary extension layer with curated, persona-tuned bundles and operator integrations. That's sold as a fractional engagement.

Yes — the shell is MIT-licensed and the source lives at github.com/rorshockbtc/greater-than. That includes the chat UI, the WebGPU model loader, the retrieval engine, the persona scaffolding, the six industry templates with their committed knowledge bundles, and the optional API server for human escalation. The proprietary layer is called Pipes — it's the curated, persona-tuned knowledge bundles and operator integrations that sit on top of the FOSS shell. Pipes is sold as a fractional engagement; the shell works honestly without it.

Pipes is the proprietary extension layer on top of the FOSS Greater shell. A Pipe is a curated knowledge bundle plus a persona-tuned configuration plus optional operator integrations, authored for a specific deployment. The Vite plugin reads any manifests in `data/pipes/` (gitignored) at build time and inlines them into the bundle; the chat widget binds at most one Pipe per persona. When no Pipe is mounted, the chat falls back to 'Generic mode' visibly — the shell never pretends to know things it doesn't. Pipes is what fractional engagements deliver; it lives at pipes.pink.

Generic mode is what you see when no Pipe is loaded for the current persona — the FOSS shell is running honestly without curated knowledge wired in. The chat header shows a 'GENERIC MODE' badge so visitors aren't misled. Mounting a Pipe under `data/pipes/` (the proprietary layer) unlocks the full Greater mode for that persona, with curated knowledge, tuned tone, and any operator integrations. The meta-bot you're talking to right now is a special case: it has a hand-curated knowledge bundle but no Pipe, because the meta-bot ships in the FOSS repo so forks get it too.

Greater is built by colonhyphenbracket — a fractional architect and engineer working in public, in pink. The author publishes long-form notes at colonhyphenbracket.pink, short-form at semi.pink, Bitcoin-flavored writing at hash.pink, and the Pipes proprietary extension layer at pipes.pink. The launch demo for the platform is the FinTech bot, which answers real Bitcoin questions in the browser with no API key and no signup, citing the Bitcoin Optech newsletter and the bitcoin-core / bitcoin-knots commit history.

items.q: Why does Greater have explicit bias toggles?
items.a: Because pretending to be neutral does damage. Every retrieval engine has a corpus, every corpus has a perspective, and a chatbot that hides that fact leaves the user with no way to evaluate the answer. Greater takes the opposite stance: every persona declares its perspective up front, and personas with multiple legitimate stances (the FinTech bot's Bitcoin Core vs Bitcoin Knots options are the canonical example) expose a bias toggle so the user can pick which lens they want the answer through. The toggle changes which subset of the corpus is privileged — but the underlying citations are always shown, so you can audit the answer.

items.q: Why won't the bot answer some questions?
items.a: Because answers that are 'almost right' are worse than 'I don't know.' Greater's engine is told to answer ONLY from the curated snippets that retrieval pulled, and to say so plainly when the snippets don't cover your question. That refusal is the feature, not a bug. If you want the bot to know about a topic, the answer is to put a chunk about that topic into its knowledge bundle — not to loosen the grounding rules. For long-tail questions the curated bundle doesn't cover, the right escape hatch is the human-escalation contact form.

When you ask a question, the engine embeds your message into a vector, scores it against the in-browser vector store of indexed chunks, pulls the top few chunks, and feeds them to the language model along with a strict instruction: cite every factual claim inline as [N] where N matches the snippet number. That's why every answer comes with a 'Sources' block underneath — those are the actual chunks the model was given. Click any citation to see the source page. If the model can't find a good match, you'll get a short 'I don't know from these sources' response instead of a confident hallucination.

The FOSS shell is free — MIT-licensed, no signup, no usage caps, no per-message billing. You can fork it and ship it on your own domain today. What costs money is the Pipes proprietary layer: curated knowledge bundles, persona-tuned configuration, and operator integrations, authored for your specific deployment. That's sold as a fractional engagement (the author works on a handful of Pipes at a time). Use the contact form on this site if you'd like to talk about scope and pricing.
